title: Contributing to hitop
text: Fork package clone onto computer. haven’t done , recommend using usethis::create_from_github("jmgirard/hitop", fork = TRUE). Install development dependencies devtools::install_dev_deps(), make sure package passes R CMD check running devtools::check(). R CMD check doesn’t pass cleanly, ’s good idea ask help continuing. Create Git branch pull request (PR). recommend using usethis::pr_init("brief-description--change"). Make changes, commit git, create PR running usethis::pr_push(), following prompts browser. title PR briefly describe change. body PR contain Fixes #issue-number. user-facing changes, add bullet top NEWS.md (.e. just first header). Follow style described https://style.tidyverse.org/news.html.

title: Scoring the HiTOP-BR
text: turn item-level ratings mean scores 7 scales, can use score_hitopbr() function. needs know object contains data columns contain item-level data. several ways can specify items. First, can provide column numbers use : shortcut. tibble, items column 3 column 47 can use items = 3:47. going also set append = FALSE can quickly see scale scores.
code: scores <- score_hitopbr(   data = ku_hitopbr,   items = 3:47,   append = FALSE ) scores #> # A tibble: 411 × 8 #>    hbr_antagonism hbr_detachment hbr_disinhibition hbr_internalizing #>             <dbl>          <dbl>             <dbl>             <dbl> #>  1           1.44           1.33              1.33              1.14 #>  2           1.33           1.33              1.33              2.43 #>  3           2.11           2.33              2.33              2.86 #>  4           1.11           1.17              1.33              1.14 #>  5           2.44           1.17              2.22              1.86 #>  6           1              1.17              1.22              1.14 #>  7           1              1                 1                 1    #>  8           1.67           1.5               1.33              1.86 #>  9           1.44           1.33              1.56              1.14 #> 10           1.33           1                 1                 1.29 #> # ℹ 401 more rows #> # ℹ 4 more variables: hbr_somatoform <dbl>, hbr_thoughtDisorder <dbl>, #> #   hbr_externalizing <dbl>, hbr_pFactor <dbl>

title: Scoring the HiTOP-BR
text: Alternatively, provide item column names character string. Typing 45 item names hassle, luckily dataset named consistently can build names automatically using sprintf(). use “hbr%02d” format apply across numbers 1 45, create zero-padded column names need. zero-padding, just used “hbr%d”.

title: Scoring the HiTOP-BR

what: Method 2: Matching via Literal Item Text
title: Scoring the HiTOP-SR
text: dataset uses completely customized column names tracked exact question prompts presented participants, use method = "text". Provide vector current column names alongside matching vector literal item text strings. Note: small mock examples contain subset full item pool, rename_hitopsr_items() safely issue cli warning letting know fewer 405 items matched. perfectly accommodates researchers intentionally administering short forms separate diagnostic modules.
code: # Setup a mock dataset with completely custom column names custom_data <- data.frame(   q_party = c(1, 2, 1),   q_flawless = c(4, 3, 4),   Age = c(21, 25, 30) )  # Define the mapping pairs my_cols <- c("q_party", "q_flawless") my_texts <- c(   "I preferred to stay home than to go to a party.",   "I felt that my work must be flawless." )  # Rename custom columns based on text matching standardized_custom <- custom_data |>   rename_hitopsr_items(     method = "text",     item_cols = my_cols,     item_text = my_texts,     prefix = "HSR_"   ) #> Warning: Only 2 out of 405 HiTOP-SR items were successfully matched and renamed. #> ℹ Note: If you plan to use `score_hitopsr()`, ensure uncollected items exist in #>   the data frame as `NA` columns.  standardized_custom #>   HSR_1 HSR_2 Age #> 1     1     4  21 #> 2     2     3  25 #> 3     1     4  30

title: Compute Cronbach's Coefficient Alpha — calc_alpha
text: Let \(k\) number items let \(X_1,\ldots,X_k\) denote item scores. Define total score \(T = \sum_{=1}^k X_i\). Cronbach's alpha can written without matrix notation $$\alpha   \;=\; \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum_{=1}^k   \mathrm{Var}(X_i)}{\mathrm{Var}(T)}\right).$$ words: take sum item variances divide variance total score; subtract ratio 1 multiply \(k/(k-1)\). \(\mathrm{Var}(X_i)\) \(\mathrm{Var}(T)\) computed using pairwise-complete observations. @section Coercion: column coerced via .numeric(). means: factors converted integer level codes (labels); characters parsable numbers become NA; dates/times become underlying numeric representations. Ensure columns truly numeric item responses pre-process needed.

title: Compute Cronbach's Coefficient Alpha — calc_alpha
